***Our Gang*** (also known as ***The Little Rascals*** or ***Hal Roach's Rascals***) is an American series of [comedy](/wiki/Comedy "Comedy") [short films](/wiki/Short_film "Short film") chronicling the adventures of a group of mischievous children in a working class neighborhood of [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"). Created by film producer [Hal Roach](/wiki/Hal_Roach "Hal Roach"), who also produced the [Laurel and Hardy](/wiki/Laurel_and_Hardy "Laurel and Hardy") films, *Our Gang* shorts were produced from 1922 to 1944, spanning the [silent film](/wiki/Silent_film "Silent film") and early [sound film](/wiki/Sound_film "Sound film") periods of [American cinema](/wiki/American_cinema "American cinema"). *Our Gang* is noted for showing children behaving in a relatively natural way; Roach and original director [Robert F. McGowan](/wiki/Robert_F._McGowan "Robert F. McGowan") worked to film the unaffected, raw nuances apparent in regular children, rather than have them imitate adult acting styles. The series also broke new ground by portraying black and white children interacting as equals during the [Jim Crow](/wiki/Jim_Crow "Jim Crow") era of [racial segregation in the United States](/wiki/Racial_segregation_in_the_United_States "Racial segregation in the United States").[[1]](#cite_note-VHS-1)

The franchise began in 1922 as a silent short subject series produced by the Roach studio and released by [Pathé Exchange](/wiki/Path%C3%A9_Exchange "Pathé Exchange"). Roach changed distributors from Pathé to [Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer](/wiki/Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer "Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer") (MGM) in 1927, and the series entered its most popular period after converting to [sound](/wiki/Sound_film "Sound film") in 1929. Production continued at Roach until 1938, when the *Our Gang* production unit was sold to MGM, where production continued until 1944. Across 220 short films and a feature-film spin-off, *[General Spanky](/wiki/General_Spanky "General Spanky")*, the *Our Gang* series featured more than 41 [child actors](/wiki/Child_actor "Child actor") as regular members of its cast.

Because MGM retained the rights to the *Our Gang* trademark after buying the series, the Roach-produced *Our Gang* sound films were re-released to theaters and syndicated for television under the title *The Little Rascals*.

The Roach-produced *Little Rascals* shorts that remain under copyright (1931–1938) are co-owned by [Legend Films](/wiki/Legend_Films "Legend Films") and Multicom Entertainment Group, using restored titles from their [Official Films](/wiki/Official_Films "Official Films") masters as "Hal Roach's Famous Kids Comedies"; the entries produced between 1922 and 1930, inclusive, are in the [public domain](/wiki/Public_domain "Public domain") in the United States. [Paramount Skydance](/wiki/Paramount_Skydance "Paramount Skydance") (through [King World Productions](/wiki/King_World_Productions "King World Productions")) owns the television distribution rights to the 1931–1938 Roach-era shorts for broadcast and cable. Meanwhile, MGM's *Our Gang* series (1938–1944) is currently owned by [Warner Bros.-Discovery](/w/index.php?title=Warner_Bros.-Discovery&action=edit&redlink=1 "Warner Bros.-Discovery (page does not exist)") through [Turner Entertainment Co.](/wiki/Turner_Entertainment "Turner Entertainment")

New productions based on the shorts have been made over the years, including the 1994 feature film *[The Little Rascals](/wiki/The_Little_Rascals_(film) "The Little Rascals (film)")*, released by [Universal Pictures](/wiki/Universal_Pictures "Universal Pictures").

## Series overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Series overview")]

Unlike many films featuring children and based in fantasy, producer/creator Hal Roach rooted *Our Gang* in real life: most of the children were poor, and the gang was often at odds with snobbish or rich children, officious adults, parents, and other such adversaries.[[1]](#cite_note-VHS-1) Although the gang members tended to avoid school, chores, and medicines like [castor oil](/wiki/Castor_oil "Castor oil") whenever possible, they would enthusiastically throw their efforts into constructing elaborate, animal-driven contraptions, putting on athletic contests and spectacles of all kinds, and generally disrupting society events and adult activities.

### Directorial approach

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Directorial approach")]

[Robert F. McGowan](/wiki/Robert_F._McGowan "Robert F. McGowan") directed most of the *Our Gang* shorts until 1933, assisted by his nephew [Anthony Mack](/wiki/Robert_A._McGowan "Robert A. McGowan"). McGowan worked to develop a style that allowed the children to be as natural as possible, downplaying the importance of the filmmaking equipment. Scripts were written by the Hal Roach comedy-writing staff, which included at various times [Leo McCarey](/wiki/Leo_McCarey "Leo McCarey"), [Frank Capra](/wiki/Frank_Capra "Frank Capra"), [Walter Lantz](/wiki/Walter_Lantz "Walter Lantz"), and [Frank Tashlin](/wiki/Frank_Tashlin "Frank Tashlin").[[2]](#cite_note-writers-2) The children, some too young to read, rarely saw the scripts; instead, McGowan would explain scenes to each child immediately before they were shot, directing the children using a [megaphone](/wiki/Megaphone "Megaphone") and encouraging [improvisation](/wiki/Improvisation "Improvisation").[[2]](#cite_note-writers-2)

With the introduction of sound films in the late 1920s, McGowan slightly modified his approach, but scripts were not closely followed until he left the series. Later *Our Gang* directors, such as [Gus Meins](/wiki/Gus_Meins "Gus Meins") and [Gordon Douglas](/wiki/Gordon_Douglas_(director) "Gordon Douglas (director)"), streamlined the approach to McGowan's methods to meet the demands of the increasingly sophisticated film industry of the mid-to-late 1930s.[[2]](#cite_note-writers-2) Douglas was forced to streamline his approach after Roach halved the running times of the shorts from two reels (20 minutes) to one reel (10 minutes).[[2]](#cite_note-writers-2)

### Finding and replacing the cast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Finding and replacing the cast")]

As children aged out of their roles, they were replaced by new children, usually from the Los Angeles area. Eventually *Our Gang* talent scouting employed large-scale national contests in which thousands of children auditioned for open roles. [Norman Chaney](/wiki/Norman_Chaney "Norman Chaney") ("Chubby"), [Matthew Beard](/wiki/Matthew_Beard_(American_actor) "Matthew Beard (American actor)") ("Stymie"), and [Billie "Buckwheat" Thomas](/wiki/Billie_%22Buckwheat%22_Thomas "Billie \"Buckwheat\" Thomas") all won contests to become members of the cast: Chaney replaced [Joe Cobb](/wiki/Joe_Cobb_(actor) "Joe Cobb (actor)"), Beard replaced [Allen Hoskins](/wiki/Allen_%22Farina%22_Hoskins "Allen \"Farina\" Hoskins") ("Farina"), and Thomas replaced Beard.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The studio was continuously bombarded by requests from parents suggesting their children for roles in the films. These children included future child stars [Mickey Rooney](/wiki/Mickey_Rooney "Mickey Rooney") and [Shirley Temple](/wiki/Shirley_Temple "Shirley Temple"), neither of whom advanced past the audition stage.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### African-American and other minority cast members

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: African-American and other minority cast members")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Babybrother01.jpg/250px-Babybrother01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Babybrother01.jpg)

The theatrical poster for the 1927 *Our Gang* comedy *[Baby Brother](/wiki/Baby_Brother "Baby Brother")*, in which [Allen "Farina" Hoskins](/wiki/Allen_Hoskins "Allen Hoskins") (center) paints a black baby with white shoe polish so that he can sell him to a lonely rich boy, [Joe Cobb](/wiki/Joe_Cobb_(actor) "Joe Cobb (actor)") (right), as a baby brother

The *Our Gang* series, produced during the [Jim Crow](/wiki/Jim_Crow "Jim Crow") era, is one of the first in cinema history in which [African Americans](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans") and [White Americans](/wiki/White_Americans "White Americans") were portrayed as equals. The five black child actors who held main roles in the series were [Ernie Morrison](/wiki/Sunshine_Sammy_Morrison "Sunshine Sammy Morrison"), [Eugene Jackson](/wiki/Eugene_Jackson "Eugene Jackson"), [Allen Hoskins](/wiki/Allen_Hoskins "Allen Hoskins"), [Matthew Beard](/wiki/Matthew_Beard_(American_actor) "Matthew Beard (American actor)") and [Billie Thomas](/wiki/Billie_%22Buckwheat%22_Thomas "Billie \"Buckwheat\" Thomas"). Morrison was the first black actor signed to a long-term contract in Hollywood history[[7]](#cite_note-7) and the first major black star in Hollywood history.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The African-American characters have often been criticized as racial stereotypes.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) The black children spoke (or were indicated as speaking via text titles in the case of the silent entries) in a stereotypical "[Negro dialect](/wiki/African-American_English "African-American English")", and several controversial gags revolved directly around their skin color, such as Stymie sweating jet-black ink[[11]](#cite_note-11) and Buckwheat contracting fake "white [measles](/wiki/Measles "Measles")" and supposedly transforming into a monkey.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) In the 1924 short *[Lodge Night](/wiki/Lodge_Night "Lodge Night"),* the kids form a parody club based on the [Ku Klux Klan](/wiki/Ku_Klux_Klan "Ku Klux Klan") (although the black children are allowed to join).[[14]](#cite_note-Lodge-14)

In their adult years, actors Morrison, Beard, and Thomas defended the series, arguing that the white characters were similarly stereotyped: the "freckle-faced kid", the "fat kid", the "neighborhood bully", the "pretty blond girl", and the "mischievous toddler". In an interview on [Tom Snyder](/wiki/Tom_Snyder "Tom Snyder")'s *[The Tomorrow Show](/wiki/The_Tomorrow_Show "The Tomorrow Show")* in 1974, Beard said of his time in the series that "I feel it was great. Some of the lines I had to say I didn't like, but I never look at it like that. I just try to look at it as mostly a fun thing. We were just a group of kids who were having fun."[[15]](#cite_note-15) In a separate interview, Morrison stated, "When it came to race, Hal Roach was [color-blind](/wiki/Color_blindness_(race) "Color blindness (race)")."[[16]](#cite_note-16)

*Our Gang's* integrated cast drew the disdain of some theater owners in the South.[[14]](#cite_note-Lodge-14) Early in film series, these owners complained to Pathé that Morrison and Hoskins were featured with too much screen time and that their prominence in the shorts would offend white audiences.[[14]](#cite_note-Lodge-14) The *Our Gang* spinoff film *[Curley](/wiki/Curley_(film) "Curley (film)")* (1947) was banned by the [Memphis, Tennessee](/wiki/Memphis,_Tennessee "Memphis, Tennessee") censor board for showing black and white children in school together, a characteristic common to even the earlier shorts.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Other minorities, including [Asian Americans](/wiki/Asian_American "Asian American") Sing Joy, Allen Tong (also known as Alan Dong), and Edward Soo Hoo, as well as [Italian-American](/wiki/Italian-American "Italian-American") actor Mickey Gubitosi (later known as [Robert Blake](/wiki/Robert_Blake_(actor) "Robert Blake (actor)")), were depicted in the series with varying levels of stereotyping.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/2/26/Our_Gang_production_still_%281923%29.jpg/250px-Our_Gang_production_still_%281923%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Our_Gang_production_still_(1923).jpg)

Left to right: [Ernie "Sunshine Sammy" Morrison](/wiki/Ernie_%22Sunshine_Sammy%22_Morrison "Ernie \"Sunshine Sammy\" Morrison"), Andy Samuel, [Allen "Farina" Hoskins](/wiki/Allen_%22Farina%22_Hoskins "Allen \"Farina\" Hoskins"), [Mickey Daniels](/wiki/Mickey_Daniels "Mickey Daniels") and [Joe Cobb](/wiki/Joe_Cobb_(actor) "Joe Cobb (actor)") in a 1923 still from one of the earliest *Our Gang* comedies

### 1922–1925: early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1922–1925: early years")]

According to Roach, he devised the idea for *Our Gang* in 1921 after auditioning a child actress whom he believed to be overly rehearsed and wearing excessive makeup. Through his window, Roach saw some children arguing over sticks of wood in a lumberyard and thought that a series of film shorts about children being themselves might be a success.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

*Our Gang* also had its roots in a canceled Roach short-subject series revolving around the adventures of a black boy called "Sunshine Sammy", played by [Ernie Morrison](/wiki/Ernie_Morrison "Ernie Morrison").[[20]](#cite_note-morrisonOurGang-20) As some theater owners had been wary of booking shorts focused on a black boy,[[20]](#cite_note-morrisonOurGang-20) the series ended after just one entry, *The Pickaninny* (1921), was produced.[[20]](#cite_note-morrisonOurGang-20) The character became a focus of the new *Our Gang* series.

Under the supervision of [Charley Chase](/wiki/Charley_Chase "Charley Chase"), work began on the first two-reel shorts in the new "kids-and-pets" series, to be called *Hal Roach's Rascals*, later that year. [Fred C. Newmeyer](/wiki/Fred_C._Newmeyer "Fred C. Newmeyer") directed the first pilot film, entitled *[Our Gang](/wiki/Our_Gang_(film) "Our Gang (film)")*, but Roach scrapped Newmeyer's work and commissioned former fireman Robert F. McGowan to reshoot the film. Roach tested it at several theaters around Hollywood to receptive audiences, and some in the press expressed a desire for additional films. The colloquial usage of the term *Our Gang* caused it to become the series' second official title, with the title cards reading "*Our Gang* Comedies: Hal Roach presents *His Rascals* in..."[[21]](#cite_note-21) The series was officially called both *Our Gang* and *Hal Roach's Rascals* until 1932, when *Our Gang* became the sole title of the series.

The first cast was recruited primarily of children recommended to Roach by studio employees, with the exception of Morrison, who was already under contract to Roach. The others included Roach photographer Gene Kornman's daughter [Mary Kornman](/wiki/Mary_Kornman "Mary Kornman"), their friends' son [Mickey Daniels](/wiki/Mickey_Daniels "Mickey Daniels"), and family friends [Allen Hoskins](/wiki/Allen_%22Farina%22_Hoskins "Allen \"Farina\" Hoskins"), [Jack Davis](/wiki/Jack_Davis_(actor) "Jack Davis (actor)"), [Jackie Condon](/wiki/Jackie_Condon "Jackie Condon"), and [Joe Cobb](/wiki/Joe_Cobb_(actor) "Joe Cobb (actor)"). Most early shorts were filmed outdoors and on location and featured a menagerie of animal characters, such as Dinah the Mule. Robert McGowan and [Tom McNamara](/wiki/Tom_McNamara_(director) "Tom McNamara (director)") served in tandem as the series' directors during this early period.

Roach's distributor [Pathé](/wiki/Path%C3%A9 "Pathé") released *[One Terrible Day](/wiki/One_Terrible_Day "One Terrible Day")*, the fourth short produced for the series, as the first short on September 10, 1922; the pilot film *[Our Gang](/wiki/Our_Gang_(film) "Our Gang (film)")* was not released until November 5. The series performed well at the box office, and by the end of the decade, the *Our Gang* children were pictured in numerous product endorsements.

The featured *Our Gang* stars were Morrison as Sunshine Sammy, Daniels, Kornman, and Hoskins as little Farina, who eventually became the most popular member of the 1920s gang[[22]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-22) and the most popular black child star of the 1920s.[[23]](#cite_note-23) A reviewer wrote of the Farina character, depicted as female although played by a male child,[[24]](#cite_note-24) in *[Photoplay](/wiki/Photoplay "Photoplay")*: "The honors go to a very young lady of color, billed as 'Little Farina.' Scarcely two years old, she goes through each set like a wee, sombre shadow."[[25]](#cite_note-25) Daniels and Kornman were very popular and were often paired in *Our Gang* and a later teen version of the series titled *[The Boy Friends](/wiki/The_Boy_Friends "The Boy Friends")*, which Roach produced from 1930 to 1932. Other early *Our Gang* children were [Eugene Jackson](/wiki/Eugene_Jackson "Eugene Jackson") as Pineapple, [Scooter Lowry](/wiki/Scooter_Lowry "Scooter Lowry"), [Andy Samuel](/wiki/Andy_Samuel "Andy Samuel"), [Johnny Downs](/wiki/Johnny_Downs "Johnny Downs"), [Winston and Weston Doty](/wiki/Winston_and_Weston_Doty "Winston and Weston Doty"), and [Jay R. Smith](/wiki/Jay_R._Smith "Jay R. Smith").

### 1926–1929: new faces and new distributors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1926–1929: new faces and new distributors")]

After Ernie, Mickey and Mary left the series in the mid-1920s, the *Our Gang* series entered a transitional period. The stress of directing child actors forced McGowan to take doctor-mandated sabbaticals for exhaustion,[[26]](#cite_note-McGowanandMeins-26) leaving his nephew [Robert A. McGowan](/wiki/Robert_A._McGowan "Robert A. McGowan") (credited as Anthony Mack) to direct many shorts in this period. The Mack-directed shorts are considered by many as among the lesser entries in the series.[[27]](#cite_note-27) New faces included [Bobby Hutchins](/wiki/Bobby_Hutchins "Bobby Hutchins") as Wheezer, [Harry Spear](/wiki/Harry_Spear "Harry Spear"), [Jean Darling](/wiki/Jean_Darling "Jean Darling") and [Mary Ann Jackson](/wiki/Mary_Ann_Jackson "Mary Ann Jackson"), while Farina served as the series' anchor.

Also at this time, the *Our Gang* cast acquired an American Pit Bull Terrier with a ring around one eye, originally named Pansy but soon known as [Pete the Pup](/wiki/Pete_the_Pup "Pete the Pup"), the most famous *Our Gang* pet. In 1927, Roach ended his distribution arrangement with the Pathé company. He agreed to release future products through the newly formed [Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer](/wiki/Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer "Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer"), which released its first *Our Gang* comedy, *[Yale vs. Harvard](/wiki/Yale_vs._Harvard "Yale vs. Harvard")*, now a [lost film](/wiki/Lost_film "Lost film"), in September 1927. The move to MGM offered Roach larger budgets and the chance for his films to be packaged with MGM features for the [Loews Theatres](/wiki/Loews_Theatres "Loews Theatres") chain.

Some shorts around this time, particularly *[Spook Spoofing](/wiki/Spook_Spoofing "Spook Spoofing")* (1928), contained extended scenes of the gang tormenting and teasing Farina, scenes that raised claims of racism that many other shorts did not warrant. These shorts marked the departure of [Jackie Condon](/wiki/Jackie_Condon "Jackie Condon"), who had been with the group from the beginning of the series.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/5/53/Jackiecooper.JPEG/250px-Jackiecooper.JPEG)](/wiki/File:Jackiecooper.JPEG)

[Jackie Cooper](/wiki/Jackie_Cooper "Jackie Cooper") in the 1930 short *[School's Out](/wiki/School%27s_Out_(1930_film) "School's Out (1930 film)")*

### 1928–1931: entering the sound era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1928–1931: entering the sound era")]

Starting in 1928, *Our Gang* comedies were distributed with [phonographic discs](/wiki/Phonograph "Phonograph") that contained synchronized music and sound-effect tracks. In the spring of 1929, the Roach studios were converted for sound recording, and *Our Gang*'s sound debut occurred in April 1929 with the 25-minute film *[Small Talk](/wiki/Small_Talk_(1929_film) "Small Talk (1929 film)")*. It took a year for McGowan and the cast to fully adjust to sound films, a period in which they lost Joe Cobb, Jean Darling and Harry Spear and added [Norman Chaney](/wiki/Norman_Chaney "Norman Chaney"), [Dorothy DeBorba](/wiki/Dorothy_DeBorba "Dorothy DeBorba"), [Matthew "Stymie" Beard](/wiki/Matthew_Beard_(American_actor) "Matthew Beard (American actor)"), [Donald Haines](/wiki/Donald_Haines "Donald Haines") and [Jackie Cooper](/wiki/Jackie_Cooper "Jackie Cooper").

Cooper proved to be the personality whom the series had been missing since Daniels left and was featured prominently in three 1930/1931 *Our Gang* films: *[Teacher's Pet](/wiki/Teacher%27s_Pet_(1930_short) "Teacher's Pet (1930 short)")*, *[School's Out](/wiki/School%27s_Out_(1930_film) "School's Out (1930 film)")*, and *[Love Business](/wiki/Love_Business "Love Business")*. These three shorts explored Cooper's crush on new schoolteacher Miss Crabtree, played by [June Marlowe](/wiki/June_Marlowe "June Marlowe"). Cooper soon won the lead role in [Paramount](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures")'s feature film *[Skippy](/wiki/Skippy_(1931_film) "Skippy (1931 film)")*, and Roach sold Cooper's contract to MGM in 1931. Other *Our Gang* members appearing in the early sound shorts included [Buddy McDonald](/wiki/Buddy_McDonald "Buddy McDonald"), [Clifton Young](/wiki/Clifton_Young "Clifton Young"), and [Shirley Jean Rickert](/wiki/Shirley_Jean_Rickert "Shirley Jean Rickert"). Many also appeared in a group [cameo appearance](/wiki/Cameo_appearance "Cameo appearance") in the all-star comedy short *[The Stolen Jools](/wiki/The_Stolen_Jools "The Stolen Jools")* (1931).

Beginning with the short *[When the Wind Blows](/wiki/When_the_Wind_Blows_(1930_film) "When the Wind Blows (1930 film)")*, in 1930 background music scores were added to the soundtracks of most of the *Our Gang* films. Initially, the music consisted of orchestral versions of popular tunes. [Marvin Hatley](/wiki/Marvin_Hatley "Marvin Hatley") had served as the music director of Hal Roach Studios since 1929, and [RCA](/wiki/RCA_Corporation "RCA Corporation") employee [Leroy Shield](/wiki/Leroy_Shield "Leroy Shield") joined the company as a part-time musical director in mid-1930. Hatley and Shield's [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz")-influenced scores, first featured in *[Pups Is Pups](/wiki/Pups_Is_Pups "Pups Is Pups") in 1930*, became recognizable trademarks of *Our Gang*, [Laurel and Hardy](/wiki/Laurel_and_Hardy "Laurel and Hardy"), and other Roach films.

Another 1930 short, *[Teacher's Pet](/wiki/Teacher%27s_Pet_(1930_film) "Teacher's Pet (1930 film)")*, marked the first use of the *Our Gang* [theme song](/wiki/Theme_song "Theme song"), ["Good Old Days"](/wiki/Good_Old_Days_(Leroy_Shield_song) "Good Old Days (Leroy Shield song)"). Originally composed by Shield for use in Laurel and Hardy's first feature, *[Pardon Us](/wiki/Pardon_Us "Pardon Us")*,[[28]](#cite_note-PardonUs-28) "Good Old Days," featuring a notable [saxophone](/wiki/Saxophone "Saxophone") solo, served as the series' theme until 1938.[[28]](#cite_note-PardonUs-28) Shield and Hatley's scores were included in the films regularly through 1934, when they became less frequent.

In 1930, Roach began production on *[The Boy Friends](/wiki/The_Boy_Friends "The Boy Friends")*, a short-subject series that was essentially a teenage version of *Our Gang*. Featuring *Our Gang* alumni Daniels and Kornman among its cast, *The Boy Friends* was produced for two years, with 15 installments in total.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/2/2f/Hi-neighbor-our-gang.jpg/250px-Hi-neighbor-our-gang.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hi-neighbor-our-gang.jpg)

The gang races rich kid [Jerry Tucker](/wiki/Jerry_Tucker_(actor) "Jerry Tucker (actor)") in their makeshift [fire engine](/wiki/Fire_apparatus "Fire apparatus") in the 1934 short *[Hi'-Neighbor!](/wiki/Hi%27-Neighbor! "Hi'-Neighbor!")*

### 1931–1933: transition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 1931–1933: transition")]

Cooper left *Our Gang* in early 1931 just before another wave of cast changes, as Farina Hoskins, Chubby Chaney, and Mary Ann Jackson all departed several months later. *Our Gang* entered another transitional period, similar to that of the mid-1920s. Matthew Beard, Wheezer Hutchins, and Dorothy DeBorba carried the series during this period, aided by [Sherwood Bailey](/wiki/Sherwood_Bailey "Sherwood Bailey") and [Kendall McComas](/wiki/Kendall_McComas "Kendall McComas"), who would play Breezy Brisbane. Unlike the mid-1920s period, McGowan sustained the quality of the series with the help of the several regular cast members and the Roach writing staff. Many of these shorts include early appearances of [Jerry Tucker](/wiki/Jerry_Tucker_(actor) "Jerry Tucker (actor)") and [Wally Albright](/wiki/Wally_Albright "Wally Albright"), who later became series regulars.

New Roach discovery [George McFarland](/wiki/Spanky_McFarland "Spanky McFarland") joined the gang as Spanky late in 1931 at the age of three and remained an *Our Gang* actor for 11 years, except for a brief break in Summer 1938. At first appearing as the tag-along toddler of the group, and later finding an accomplice in [Scotty Beckett](/wiki/Scotty_Beckett "Scotty Beckett") in 1934, Spanky quickly became *Our Gang*'s greatest child star. He won parts in a number of outside features, appeared in many *Our Gang* product endorsements and spinoff merchandise items, and popularized the expressions "Okey-dokey!" and "Okey-doke!"[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Veteran child actor [Dickie Moore](/wiki/Dickie_Moore_(actor) "Dickie Moore (actor)") joined in the middle of 1932 and remained with the series for one year. Other members in these years included Mary Ann Jackson's brother Dickie Jackson, [John "Uh-huh" Collum](/wiki/John_Collum "John Collum"), and [Tommy Bond](/wiki/Tommy_Bond "Tommy Bond"). Upon Moore's departure in mid 1933, long-term *Our Gang* members such as Wheezer (who had been with *Our Gang* since the late Pathé silents period) and Dorothy left the series as well.

### 1933–1936: new directions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 1933–1936: new directions")]

McGowan, exhausted from the stress of working with the child actors, had as early as 1931 tried to resign as producer/director of *Our Gang*.[[26]](#cite_note-McGowanandMeins-26) Lacking a replacement, Roach persuaded him to remain for another year.[[26]](#cite_note-McGowanandMeins-26) At the start of the 1933–34 season, the *Our Gang* series format was significantly altered to accommodate McGowan and persuade him to stay another year.[[26]](#cite_note-McGowanandMeins-26) The first two entries of the season in Fall 1933, *[Bedtime Worries](/wiki/Bedtime_Worries "Bedtime Worries")* and *[Wild Poses](/wiki/Wild_Poses "Wild Poses")* (which featured a cameo by Laurel and Hardy), focused on Spanky and his hapless parents, portrayed by [Gay Seabrook](/wiki/Gay_Seabrook "Gay Seabrook") and [Emerson Treacy](/wiki/Emerson_Treacy "Emerson Treacy"), in a family-oriented [situation-comedy](/wiki/Situation_comedy "Situation comedy") format similar to the style later popular on [television](/wiki/Television "Television"). A smaller cast of *Our Gang* kids—Matthew Beard, Tommy Bond, Jerry Tucker, and Georgie Billings—were featured in supporting roles with reduced screen time.

Unsatisfied, McGowan abruptly departed after *[Wild Poses](/wiki/Wild_Poses "Wild Poses")* (1933). *Our Gang* entered a four-month hiatus, during which the series was revised to a format similar to its original style, and German-born [Gus Meins](/wiki/Gus_Meins "Gus Meins") was hired as the new director.[[26]](#cite_note-McGowanandMeins-26)

*[Hi-Neighbor!](/wiki/Hi-Neighbor! "Hi-Neighbor!")*, released in March 1934, ended the hiatus and was the first series entry directed by Meins, a veteran of the formerly competing *[Buster Brown](/wiki/Buster_Brown "Buster Brown")* short-subject series. [Gordon Douglas](/wiki/Gordon_Douglas_(director) "Gordon Douglas (director)") served as Meins's assistant director, and Fred Newmeyer alternated directorial duties with Meins for a handful of shorts. Meins's *Our Gang* shorts were less improvisational than were McGowan's and featured a heavier reliance on dialogue.[[30]](#cite_note-30) McGowan returned two years later to direct his final *Our Gang* film *[Divot Diggers](/wiki/Divot_Diggers "Divot Diggers")*, released in 1936.

Retaining McFarland, Beard, Bond, and Tucker, the revised series added [Scotty Beckett](/wiki/Scotty_Beckett "Scotty Beckett"), [Wally Albright](/wiki/Wally_Albright "Wally Albright"), and [Billie Thomas](/wiki/Billie_Thomas "Billie Thomas"), who soon began playing the character of Stymie's sister "Buckwheat", although Thomas was male. Semiregular actors such as [Jackie Lynn Taylor](/wiki/Jackie_Lynn_Taylor "Jackie Lynn Taylor"), [Marianne Edwards](/wiki/Marianne_Edwards "Marianne Edwards"), and [Leonard Kibrick](/wiki/Leonard_Kibrick "Leonard Kibrick") as the neighborhood bully, joined the series. Bond and Albright left in the middle of 1934; Taylor and Edwards would depart by 1935.

Early in 1935, new cast members [Carl Switzer](/wiki/Carl_%22Alfalfa%22_Switzer "Carl \"Alfalfa\" Switzer") and his brother [Harold](/wiki/Harold_Switzer "Harold Switzer") joined *Our Gang* after impressing Roach with an impromptu musical performance at the studio [commissary](/wiki/Cafeteria "Cafeteria"). While Harold would eventually be relegated to the role of a background player, Carl, nicknamed "Alfalfa", eventually replaced Beckett as Spanky's sidekick. Beard as Stymie left the cast soon after, and the Buckwheat character morphed subtly into a male. That same year, [Darla Hood](/wiki/Darla_Hood "Darla Hood"), Patsy May, and [Eugene Lee](/wiki/Eugene_Gordon_Lee "Eugene Gordon Lee") as Porky joined the gang. Beckett departed for a career in features but returned in 1939 for two shorts, *[Cousin Wilbur](/wiki/Cousin_Wilbur "Cousin Wilbur")* and *[Dog Daze](/wiki/Dog_Daze_(1939_film) "Dog Daze (1939 film)")*.

### The final Roach years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: The final Roach years")]

*Our Gang* was very successful during the 1920s and the early 1930s. However, by 1934, many theater owners were increasingly dropping two-reel (20-minute) comedies such as *Our Gang* and the *Laurel & Hardy* series and running [double-feature](/wiki/Double_feature "Double feature") programs instead. The Laurel and Hardy series, formerly film shorts, became features exclusively in mid-1935. By 1936, Hal Roach began debating plans to discontinue *Our Gang* until [Louis B. Mayer](/wiki/Louis_B._Mayer "Louis B. Mayer"), head of Roach's distributor MGM, persuaded Roach to keep the popular series in production.[[31]](#cite_note-onereel-31) Roach agreed, producing shorter, one-reel *Our Gang* comedies (10 minutes in length instead of 20). The first one-reel *Our Gang* short, *[Bored of Education](/wiki/Bored_of_Education "Bored of Education")* (1936), marked the *Our Gang* directorial debut of former assistant director Gordon Douglas and won the [Academy Award for Best Short Subject (One Reel)](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Live_Action_Short_Film "Academy Award for Live Action Short Film") in 1937.

As part of the arrangement with MGM to continue *Our Gang*, Roach received clearance to produce an *Our Gang* feature film, *[General Spanky](/wiki/General_Spanky "General Spanky")*, hoping that he might move the series to features as was done with Laurel and Hardy.[[31]](#cite_note-onereel-31) Directed by Gordon Douglas and Fred Newmeyer, *General Spanky* featured characters Spanky, Buckwheat, and Alfalfa in a sentimental, Shirley Temple-style story set during the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"). The film focused more on the adult leads ([Phillips Holmes](/wiki/Phillips_Holmes "Phillips Holmes") and [Rosina Lawrence](/wiki/Rosina_Lawrence "Rosina Lawrence")) than the children and was a box-office disappointment.[[32]](#cite_note-32) No further *Our Gang* features were produced.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Ogfolliesof38.jpg/250px-Ogfolliesof38.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ogfolliesof38.jpg)

[George McFarland](/wiki/Spanky_McFarland "Spanky McFarland"), [Darla Hood](/wiki/Darla_Hood "Darla Hood"), and [Carl "Alfalfa" Switzer](/wiki/Carl_Switzer "Carl Switzer") in the "Club Spanky" dream sequence from the 1937 short *[Our Gang Follies of 1938](/wiki/Our_Gang_Follies_of_1938 "Our Gang Follies of 1938").*

After years of gradual cast changes, the troupe standardized in 1936 with the move to one-reel shorts. The 1936–1939 incarnation of the cast is perhaps the best-known of the series, featuring Spanky, Alfalfa, Darla, Buckwheat, and Porky, with recurring characters such as neighborhood bullies Butch and Woim and the bookworm Waldo. Bond, an intermittent member of the gang since 1932, returned as Butch beginning with the 1937 short *[Glove Taps](/wiki/Glove_Taps "Glove Taps")*. [Sidney Kibrick](/wiki/Sidney_Kibrick "Sidney Kibrick"), the younger brother of Leonard Kibrick, played Butch's crony Woim.

*Glove Taps* also featured the first appearance of [Darwood Kaye](/wiki/Darwood_%22Waldo%22_Kaye "Darwood \"Waldo\" Kaye") as the bespectacled, foppish Waldo. In later shorts, both Butch and Waldo were portrayed as Alfalfa's rivals in his pursuit of Darla's affections. Other popular elements in these mid-to-late-1930s shorts include the "He-Man Woman Haters Club" from *[Hearts Are Thumps](/wiki/Hearts_Are_Thumps "Hearts Are Thumps")* and *[Mail and Female](/wiki/Mail_and_Female "Mail and Female")* (both 1937), the Laurel and Hardy-style interaction between Alfalfa and Spanky, and the comic tag-along team of Porky and Buckwheat.

Roach produced the final two-reel *Our Gang* short, a high-budget musical special entitled *[Our Gang Follies of 1938](/wiki/Our_Gang_Follies_of_1938 "Our Gang Follies of 1938")*, in 1937 as a [parody](/wiki/Parody "Parody") of MGM's *[Broadway Melody of 1938](/wiki/Broadway_Melody_of_1938 "Broadway Melody of 1938")*. Alfalfa, who aspires to be an [opera](/wiki/Opera "Opera") singer, falls asleep and dreams that his old pal Spanky has become the rich owner of a swanky [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_(Manhattan) "Broadway (Manhattan)") nightclub where Darla and Buckwheat perform, making "hundreds and thousands of dollars".

As the profit margins continued to decline because of double features,[[33]](#cite_note-33) Roach could no longer afford to continue producing *Our Gang*. The lack of consistent success with Roach's concurrent program of feature output and an ultimately unsuccessful partnership with producer [Vittorio Mussolini](/wiki/Vittorio_Mussolini "Vittorio Mussolini"), son of Italian dictator [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini"), caused disagreements with the management at MGM and its parent company, [Loews Inc.](/wiki/Loews_Inc. "Loews Inc."), which elected to end MGM's partnership with Roach.[[34]](#cite_note-34) However, MGM did not want *Our Gang* discontinued and agreed to assume production.

On May 31, 1938, Roach sold the *Our Gang* unit to MGM, including the rights to the name and the contracts for the actors and writers, for $25,000 (equal to $571,809 today).[[35]](#cite_note-MGMsale-35) After delivering the Laurel and Hardy feature *[Block-Heads](/wiki/Block-Heads "Block-Heads")*, Roach signed a new distribution deal with [United Artists](/wiki/United_Artists "United Artists") and left the short-subjects business. The final Roach-produced short in the *Our Gang* series, *[Hide and Shriek](/wiki/Hide_and_Shriek "Hide and Shriek")*, was his final short-subject production.

### The MGM era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: The MGM era")]

*[The Little Ranger](/wiki/The_Little_Ranger "The Little Ranger")* was the first *Our Gang* short to be produced at MGM. Gordon Douglas was loaned from Hal Roach Studios to direct *The Little Ranger* and another early MGM short, *Aladdin's Lantern*, while MGM assigned [George Sidney](/wiki/George_Sidney "George Sidney"), a young director from its own shorts department,[[36]](#cite_note-36) as the permanent series director. *Our Gang* would be used by MGM as a training ground for future feature directors: Sidney, [Edward Cahn](/wiki/Edward_Cahn_(director) "Edward Cahn (director)") and [Cy Endfield](/wiki/Cy_Endfield "Cy Endfield") all worked on *Our Gang* before advancing to feature films. Herbert Glazer remained a [second-unit](/wiki/Second_unit "Second unit") director outside of his work on the series.

Nearly all of the 52 MGM-produced *Our Gang* films were written by former Roach director Hal Law and former junior director Robert A. McGowan, nephew of former senior *Our Gang* director Robert F. McGowan, who was credited for these shorts as Robert McGowan (although he is also known as Anthony Mack), causing confusion for audiences and critics.

The last few Roach comedies and the first few MGM comedies featured Carl "Alfalfa" Switzer as the lead character, as George "Spanky" McFarland had departed from the series when his contract expired in March 1938.[[37]](#cite_note-37) Casting his replacement was delayed until after the move to MGM, which opted to rehire McFarland to continue with the series.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

In 1939, Mickey Gubitosi (later known by the stage name of [Robert Blake](/wiki/Robert_Blake_(actor) "Robert Blake (actor)")) replaced Eugene "Porky" Lee, who had matured too quickly.[[39]](#cite_note-Maltin_&_Bann,_p._211-39) Tommy Bond, Darwood Kaye, and Carl Switzer all left the series in 1940, and [Billy "Froggy" Laughlin](/wiki/Billy_%22Froggy%22_Laughlin "Billy \"Froggy\" Laughlin") (with his [Popeye](/wiki/Popeye "Popeye")-style trick voice) and [Janet Burston](/wiki/Janet_Burston "Janet Burston") were added to the cast. By the end of 1941, Darla Hood had departed from the series, and George McFarland followed her within a year. Billie Thomas remained in the cast as Buckwheat until the end of the series as the sole holdover from the Roach era.

The MGM *Our Gang* films were not received as favorably as were the Roach-produced shorts, largely because of MGM's inexperience with *Our Gang*'s style of [slapstick comedy](/wiki/Slapstick_comedy "Slapstick comedy") and its insistence on retaining Alfalfa, Spanky, and Buckwheat in the series as they became teenagers.[[39]](#cite_note-Maltin_&_Bann,_p._211-39) The MGM entries are thus considered by many film historians, and the *Our Gang* children themselves, as lesser films than the Roach entries.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The children's performances were criticized as stilted and stiff, their dialogue recited instead of spoken naturally. Adult situations often drove the action, with each film often incorporating a moral, a civics lesson, or, as the United States prepared for and then entered [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), a patriotic theme.[[39]](#cite_note-Maltin_&_Bann,_p._211-39) The series was given a new setting in the fictitious town of Greenpoint rather than the gritty Los Angeles neighborhoods where the Hal Roach productions had been filmed and set. The mayhem caused by the kids was significantly muted.

Exhibitors noticed the drop in quality and often complained that the series was slipping. When six of the 13 shorts released between 1942 and 1943 sustained losses rather than turning profits,[[41]](#cite_note-41) MGM discontinued *Our Gang*.[[42]](#cite_note-42) The final three *Our Gang* shorts were all directed by Cy Endfield in late 1943 and released the following spring. *[Tale of a Dog](/wiki/Tale_of_a_Dog "Tale of a Dog")* was released as part of the *MGM Miniatures* series on April 15, 1944. The other two shorts, released to close out the regular *Our Gang* series, were *[Radio Bugs](/wiki/Radio_Bugs "Radio Bugs")*, released on April 1, 1944, and the final *Our Gang* comedy, *[Dancing Romeo](/wiki/Dancing_Romeo "Dancing Romeo")*, released on April 29, 1944.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

Since 1937, *Our Gang* had been featured as a licensed [comic strip](/wiki/Comic_strip "Comic strip") in the British [comic](/wiki/Comic_book "Comic book") *[The Dandy](/wiki/The_Dandy "The Dandy")*, illustrated by [Dudley D. Watkins](/wiki/Dudley_D._Watkins "Dudley D. Watkins"). Starting in 1942, MGM licensed *Our Gang* to American publisher [Dell Comics](/wiki/Dell_Comics "Dell Comics") for the publication of *Our Gang Comics*, featuring the gang alongside MGM cartoon characters such as [Barney Bear](/wiki/Barney_Bear "Barney Bear") and [Tom and Jerry](/wiki/Tom_and_Jerry "Tom and Jerry").[[44]](#cite_note-44) The strips in *The Dandy* ended three years after the demise of the *Our Gang* shorts in 1947. *Our Gang Comics* outlasted the series by five years, changing its name to *Tom and Jerry Comics* in 1949. In 2006, [Fantagraphics Books](/wiki/Fantagraphics_Books "Fantagraphics Books") issued a series of volumes reprinting the *Our Gang* stories, most of them written and drawn by *[Pogo](/wiki/Pogo_(comic_strip) "Pogo (comic strip)")* creator [Walt Kelly](/wiki/Walt_Kelly "Walt Kelly").

## Later years and *The Little Rascals* revival

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Later years and The Little Rascals revival")]

### *The Little Rascals* television package

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: The Little Rascals television package")]

When Roach sold *Our Gang* to MGM, he retained the option to buy the rights to the *Our Gang* trademark, provided that he produced no more children's comedies in the *Our Gang* vein. In the late 1940s, he created a new film property in the *Our Gang* mold and forfeited his right to buy the name *Our Gang* in order to obtain permission to produce two [Cinecolor](/wiki/Cinecolor "Cinecolor") featurettes, *[Curley](/wiki/Curley_(1947_film) "Curley (1947 film)")* and *[Who Killed Doc Robbin](/wiki/Who_Killed_Doc_Robbin "Who Killed Doc Robbin")*. Neither film was critically or financially successful, and Roach turned to rereleasing the original *Our Gang* comedies.

In 1949, MGM sold the back catalog of 1927–1938 *Our Gang* silent and talking shorts to Roach while retaining the rights to the *Our Gang* name, the 52 *Our Gang* films that it produced, and the feature *General Spanky*. Under the terms of the sale, Roach was required to remove the [Leo the Lion](/wiki/Leo_the_Lion_(MGM) "Leo the Lion (MGM)") studio logo and all instances of the names or logos "Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer", "Loew's Incorporated", and "Our Gang" from the reissued film prints. Using a modified version of the series' original name, Roach repackaged 79 of the 80 sound *Our Gang* shorts as *The Little Rascals*. [Monogram Pictures](/wiki/Monogram_Pictures "Monogram Pictures") and its successor, Allied Artists, reissued the films to theaters beginning in 1950.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Allied Artists' television department, Interstate Television, syndicated the films to television in 1954.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48)

Under its new name, *The Little Rascals* enjoyed renewed popularity on television, and new *Little Rascals* comic books, toys, and other licensed merchandise were produced. As was the custom at the time with theatrical film productions rebroadcast on television, no actors appearing in the films were paid [residuals](/wiki/Residual_(entertainment_industry) "Residual (entertainment industry)") for the revival.[[49]](#cite_note-atlantic-49)[[50]](#cite_note-MaltinBannSpanky-50) MGM prepared to distribute its own *Our Gang* shorts to television in 1957,[[51]](#cite_note-51) and offers for the shorts to stations were issued beginning in 1958.[[52]](#cite_note-52) The two separate packages of *Our Gang* films competed with each other in [syndication](/wiki/Television_syndication "Television syndication") for three decades. Some stations bought both packages and played them alongside each other under the *Little Rascals* show banner.

The television rights to the silent [Pathé](/wiki/Path%C3%A9 "Pathé") *Our Gang* comedies were sold to National Telepix and other distributors, who distributed the films under titles such as *The Mischief Makers* and *Those Lovable Scallawags with Their Gangs*.

### King World's acquisition and edits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: King World's acquisition and edits")]

In 1963, Hal Roach Studios, operated by Roach's son Hal Jr., filed for bankruptcy. Struggling novice syndication agent Charles King purchased the television rights to *The Little Rascals* in the bankruptcy proceedings and returned the shorts to television. The success of *The Little Rascals* paved the way for King's new company, [King World Productions](/wiki/King_World_Productions "King World Productions"), to become one of the largest television syndicators in the world. [Paramount Global](/wiki/Paramount_Global "Paramount Global"), King World's latest successor, currently owns distribution rights.

During the 1950s and 1960s, the [NAACP](/wiki/NAACP "NAACP"), An American [civil rights](/wiki/Civil_rights "Civil rights") organization working on behalf of African-Americans, protested against the racial humor and the stereotyping of the black characters in the *Our Gang* films included in the *Little Rascals* TV package.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELee2015210–211,_227-53) After an NAACP campaign to remove the syndication package from the air began gaining traction and finding success with some local television stations, King World permitted the NAACP to supervise edits of the shorts in the *Little Rascals* package in 1971.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELee2015210–211,_227-53) Significant edits were performed, removing content deemed racist, stereotypical, or in generally poor taste.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELee2015210–211,_227-53) Many series entries were trimmed by two to four minutes, while others such as *[Spanky](/wiki/Spanky_(film) "Spanky (film)")*, *[Bargain Day](/wiki/Bargain_Day "Bargain Day")*, *[The Pinch Singer](/wiki/The_Pinch_Singer "The Pinch Singer")* and *[Mush and Milk](/wiki/Mush_and_Milk "Mush and Milk")* were cut to nearly half of their original lengths.[[54]](#cite_note-MaltinBannNAACP-54)

At the same time, eight *Little Rascals* shorts were removed from the King World television package altogether. *[Lazy Days](/wiki/Lazy_Days_(film) "Lazy Days (film)")*, *[Moan and Groan, Inc.](/wiki/Moan_and_Groan,_Inc. "Moan and Groan, Inc.")*, *[A Tough Winter](/wiki/A_Tough_Winter "A Tough Winter")* (featuring [Stepin Fetchit](/wiki/Stepin_Fetchit "Stepin Fetchit")), *[Little Daddy](/wiki/Little_Daddy "Little Daddy")*, *[A Lad an' a Lamp](/wiki/A_Lad_an%27_a_Lamp "A Lad an' a Lamp")*, *[The Kid From Borneo](/wiki/The_Kid_From_Borneo "The Kid From Borneo")*, and *[Little Sinner](/wiki/Little_Sinner "Little Sinner")* were deleted from the syndication package because of purportedly racial material and stereotyping (mostly concerning African-Americans, although *Moan and Groan, Inc.* was also deleted because of [Max Davidson](/wiki/Max_Davidson "Max Davidson")'s [Jewish-American](/wiki/Jewish-American "Jewish-American") character).[[54]](#cite_note-MaltinBannNAACP-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55) *[Big Ears](/wiki/Big_Ears_(film) "Big Ears (film)")* was deleted for its content dealing with divorce and its depiction of children carelessly ingesting drugs from a medicine cabinet. The early talkie *[Railroadin'](/wiki/Railroadin%27 "Railroadin'")* was never part of the television package because its soundtrack (recorded on [Vitaphone](/wiki/Vitaphone "Vitaphone") phonographic records) was considered lost, although the soundtrack was found in the MGM vault in the late 1970s and restored to the film.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

[Turner Entertainment Co.](/wiki/Turner_Entertainment "Turner Entertainment") acquired the pre-May 1986 MGM library in 1986, and the 1938–1944 MGM-produced *Our Gang* shorts were shown on Turner's [TBS](/wiki/TBS_(American_TV_channel) "TBS (American TV channel)") and [TNT](/wiki/TNT_(American_TV_network) "TNT (American TV network)") cable networks for many years as early-morning programming filler, with a regular slot on Sundays at 6 a.m. ET on TNT.

In the early 2000s, the 71 films in the King World package were reedited, reinstating many, but not all, of the edits performed in 1971 and the original *Our Gang* title cards. These new television prints debuted on [AMC](/wiki/AMC_(TV_channel) "AMC (TV channel)") in 2001 and ran until 2003.

### New *Little Rascals* productions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: New Little Rascals productions")]

Many producers, including *Our Gang* alumnus [Jackie Cooper](/wiki/Jackie_Cooper "Jackie Cooper"), created pilots for new *Little Rascals* television series, but none ever went into production.

In 1977, [Norman Lear](/wiki/Norman_Lear "Norman Lear") tried to revive the franchise, taping three pilot episodes of *The Little Rascals*. The pilots were not bought, but were notable for including [Gary Coleman](/wiki/Gary_Coleman "Gary Coleman").

1979 brought *[The Little Rascals Christmas Special](/wiki/The_Little_Rascals_Christmas_Special "The Little Rascals Christmas Special")*, an animated Christmas special produced by Murakami-Wolf-Swenson, written by [Romeo Muller](/wiki/Romeo_Muller "Romeo Muller") and featuring the voice work of Darla Hood (who died suddenly before the special aired) and Matthew "Stymie" Beard.

From 1982 to 1984, [Hanna-Barbera Productions](/wiki/Hanna-Barbera_Productions "Hanna-Barbera Productions") produced a [Saturday-morning cartoon](/wiki/Saturday_morning_cartoon "Saturday morning cartoon") [version of *The Little Rascals*](/wiki/The_Little_Rascals_(animated_TV_series) "The Little Rascals (animated TV series)"), which aired on ABC during *[The Pac-Man/Little Rascals/Richie Rich Show](/wiki/The_Pac-Man/Little_Rascals/Richie_Rich_Show "The Pac-Man/Little Rascals/Richie Rich Show")* (later *The Monchichis/Little Rascals/Richie Rich Show*).[[57]](#cite_note-McNeil3-57) It starred the voices of [Patty Maloney](/wiki/Patty_Maloney "Patty Maloney") as Darla; [Peter Cullen](/wiki/Peter_Cullen "Peter Cullen") as Petey and Officer Ed; [Scott Menville](/wiki/Scott_Menville "Scott Menville") as Spanky; [Julie McWhirter Dees](/wiki/Julie_McWhirter "Julie McWhirter") as Alfalfa, Porky and The Woim; [Shavar Ross](/wiki/Shavar_Ross "Shavar Ross") as Buckwheat, and [B.J. Ward](/wiki/B._J._Ward_(actress) "B. J. Ward (actress)") as Butch and Waldo.

In 1994, [Amblin Entertainment](/wiki/Amblin_Entertainment "Amblin Entertainment") and [Universal Pictures](/wiki/Universal_Pictures "Universal Pictures") released *[The Little Rascals](/wiki/The_Little_Rascals_(film) "The Little Rascals (film)")*, a feature film based loosely on the series and featuring interpretations of classic *Our Gang* shorts, including *Hearts are Thumps*, *Rushin' Ballet*, and *Hi'-Neighbor!* The film, directed by [Penelope Spheeris](/wiki/Penelope_Spheeris "Penelope Spheeris"), stars Travis Tedford as Spanky, [Bug Hall](/wiki/Bug_Hall "Bug Hall") as Alfalfa, and [Ross Bagley](/wiki/Ross_Bagley "Ross Bagley") as Buckwheat, with cameos by the [Olsen twins](/wiki/Mary-Kate_and_Ashley_Olsen "Mary-Kate and Ashley Olsen"), [Whoopi Goldberg](/wiki/Whoopi_Goldberg "Whoopi Goldberg"), [Mel Brooks](/wiki/Mel_Brooks "Mel Brooks"), [Reba McEntire](/wiki/Reba_McEntire "Reba McEntire"), [Daryl Hannah](/wiki/Daryl_Hannah "Daryl Hannah"), [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") and [Raven-Symoné](/wiki/Raven-Symon%C3%A9 "Raven-Symoné").[[58]](#cite_note-auto-58) *The Little Rascals* was a moderate success for Universal, returning $51,764,950 at the [box office](/wiki/Box_office "Box office").[[58]](#cite_note-auto-58)

In 2014, [Universal Pictures](/wiki/Universal_Pictures "Universal Pictures") released the direct-to-video film *[The Little Rascals Save the Day](/wiki/The_Little_Rascals_Save_the_Day "The Little Rascals Save the Day")*, a second film loosely based on the series and featuring interpretations of classic *Our Gang* shorts, including *[Helping Grandma](/wiki/Helping_Grandma "Helping Grandma")*, *[Mike Fright](/wiki/Mike_Fright "Mike Fright")*, and *[Birthday Blues](/wiki/Birthday_Blues "Birthday Blues")*. The film was directed by [Alex Zamm](/wiki/Alex_Zamm "Alex Zamm") and stars [Jet Jurgensmeyer](/wiki/Jet_Jurgensmeyer "Jet Jurgensmeyer") as Spanky, Drew Justice as Alfalfa, [Eden Wood](/wiki/Eden_Wood "Eden Wood") as Darla, and [Doris Roberts](/wiki/Doris_Roberts "Doris Roberts") as the kids' adopted grandmother.

## Legacy and influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Legacy and influence")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/FourColorComicsNo674.jpg/250px-FourColorComicsNo674.jpg)](/wiki/File:FourColorComicsNo674.jpg)

Painted cover to *[Four Color Comics](/wiki/Four_Color_Comics "Four Color Comics")* number 674, featuring "The Little Rascals" ([Dell](/wiki/Dell_Comics "Dell Comics"), January 1956). Artist: David Gantz.

The characters in this series are well-known cultural icons, often identified solely by their first names. The characters of Alfalfa, Spanky, Buckwheat, Porky, Darla, Froggy, Butch, Woim, and Waldo were especially well known. As with many child actors, the *Our Gang* children were [typecast](/wiki/Typecasting_(acting) "Typecasting (acting)") and had trouble outgrowing their *Our Gang* images.

Several *Our Gang* alumni, including [Carl "Alfalfa" Switzer](/wiki/Carl_%22Alfalfa%22_Switzer "Carl \"Alfalfa\" Switzer"), [Scotty Beckett](/wiki/Scotty_Beckett "Scotty Beckett"), [Norman "Chubby" Chaney](/wiki/Norman_%22Chubby%22_Chaney "Norman \"Chubby\" Chaney"), [Billy "Froggy" Laughlin](/wiki/Billy_Laughlin "Billy Laughlin"), [Donald Haines](/wiki/Donald_Haines "Donald Haines"), [Bobby "Wheezer" Hutchins](/wiki/Bobby_%22Wheezer%22_Hutchins "Bobby \"Wheezer\" Hutchins"), [Darla Hood](/wiki/Darla_Hood "Darla Hood"), [Matthew "Stymie" Beard](/wiki/Matthew_%22Stymie%22_Beard "Matthew \"Stymie\" Beard"), [Billie "Buckwheat" Thomas](/wiki/Billie_%22Buckwheat%22_Thomas "Billie \"Buckwheat\" Thomas"), and [George "Spanky" McFarland](/wiki/George_%22Spanky%22_McFarland "George \"Spanky\" McFarland"), died before age 65, in some cases well earlier. This led to rumors of an *Our Gang/Little Rascals* "curse", rumors further popularized by a 2002 *[E! True Hollywood Story](/wiki/E!_True_Hollywood_Story "E! True Hollywood Story")* documentary entitled "The Curse of the Little Rascals".[[59]](#cite_note-59) The [Snopes.com](/wiki/Snopes.com "Snopes.com") website debunks the rumor of an *Our Gang* curse, stating that there was no pattern of unusual deaths when taking all of the major *Our Gang* stars into account, despite the deaths of a select few.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

The children's work in the series was largely unrewarded in later years, although McFarland was awarded a star on the [Hollywood Walk of Fame](/wiki/Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame "Hollywood Walk of Fame") posthumously in 1994. No *Our Gang* children received any [residuals](/wiki/Residual_(entertainment_industry) "Residual (entertainment industry)") or [royalties](/wiki/Royalties "Royalties") from [reruns](/wiki/Rerun "Rerun") of the shorts or licensed products with their likenesses. Their only pay was their weekly salaries when filming the shorts, ranging from $40 a week for newcomers to $200 or more weekly for stars such as Farina, Spanky, and Alfalfa.[[22]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-22)

Only a handful of *Our Gang* alumni sustained acting careers outside the series. [Ernie Morrison](/wiki/Ernie_Morrison "Ernie Morrison") appeared in several films as a member the *[East Side Kids](/wiki/East_Side_Kids "East Side Kids")* in the early 1940s. [Jackie Cooper](/wiki/Jackie_Cooper "Jackie Cooper") had a long and successful career in feature films and television as an adolescent and adult actor, and later as a producer and director while pursuing a parallel career as a [U.S. Navy](/wiki/U.S._Navy "U.S. Navy") officer. Cooper is especially known today for portraying [Perry White](/wiki/Perry_White "Perry White") in the 1978–1987 *[Superman](/wiki/Superman_film_series "Superman film series")* movies, and for directing episodes of TV series such as *[M\*A\*S\*H](/wiki/M*A*S*H_(TV_series) "M*A*S*H (TV series)")* and *[Superboy](/wiki/Superboy_(TV_series) "Superboy (TV series)")*. [Dickie Moore](/wiki/Dickie_Moore_(actor) "Dickie Moore (actor)"), who had been a child actor before joining *Our Gang*, continued to perform child and teenage roles, appearing in over 100 films and television episodes, including co-starring with [Shirley Temple](/wiki/Shirley_Temple "Shirley Temple") in *[Miss Annie Rooney](/wiki/Miss_Annie_Rooney "Miss Annie Rooney")* (1942) and as "The Kid" in the [film noir](/wiki/Film_noir "Film noir") classic *[Out of the Past](/wiki/Out_of_the_Past "Out of the Past")* (1947). [Scotty Beckett](/wiki/Scotty_Beckett "Scotty Beckett") had dozens of film and television roles until the mid-1950s, including playing the young [Al Jolson](/wiki/Al_Jolson "Al Jolson") in *[The Jolson Story](/wiki/The_Jolson_Story "The Jolson Story")* (1946). [Tommy Bond](/wiki/Tommy_Bond "Tommy Bond") had a number of later film roles, including playing [Jimmy Olsen](/wiki/Jimmy_Olsen "Jimmy Olsen") in two 1940s *[Superman](/wiki/Superman_(serial) "Superman (serial)")* serials. Mickey Gubitosi later became [Robert Blake](/wiki/Robert_Blake_(actor) "Robert Blake (actor)") and found great success in the 1960s and 1970s as an actor, particularly known for *[In Cold Blood](/wiki/In_Cold_Blood_(film) "In Cold Blood (film)")* (1967) and the television series *[Baretta](/wiki/Baretta "Baretta")* (1975–78), which netted him an [Emmy Award](/wiki/Emmy_Award "Emmy Award").

[Mickey Daniels](/wiki/Mickey_Daniels "Mickey Daniels") and [Mary Kornman](/wiki/Mary_Kornman "Mary Kornman") became two of the leads of *[The Boy Friends](/wiki/The_Boy_Friends "The Boy Friends")*, an early sound (1930–32) Hal Roach short-subjects series pitched as a college-age version of *Our Gang*; both eventually left acting for other careers. [Stymie Beard](/wiki/Stymie_Beard "Stymie Beard") continued working as an actor in both Hollywood films and independent "[race films](/wiki/Race_film "Race film")" for African-American audiences until falling into [heroin](/wiki/Heroin "Heroin") addiction in the 1940s as a young adult.[[61]](#cite_note-mbeard_imdb_bio-61) He made a comeback in the 1970s as a middle-aged adult character actor on TV shows such as *[Good Times](/wiki/Good_Times "Good Times")* (1974–79) and films such as *[Truck Turner](/wiki/Truck_Turner "Truck Turner")* (1974) and *[The Buddy Holly Story](/wiki/The_Buddy_Holly_Story "The Buddy Holly Story")* (1978) following several years in rehab.[[61]](#cite_note-mbeard_imdb_bio-61) Spanky McFarland had a notable run of supporting parts in outside Hollywood studio features while still in *Our Gang*, including supporting parts in *[Kentucky Kernels](/wiki/Kentucky_Kernels "Kentucky Kernels")* (1934) and *[The Trail of the Lonesome Pine](/wiki/The_Trail_of_the_Lonesome_Pine_(1936_film) "The Trail of the Lonesome Pine (1936 film)")* (1936). However, McFarland left acting as a teenager following World War II. Several of the other former cast members had supporting or bit roles in later film or television productions, including Farina Hoskins (*[You Said a Mouthful](/wiki/You_Said_a_Mouthful "You Said a Mouthful")*, 1931), Alfalfa Switzer (*[It's a Wonderful Life](/wiki/It%27s_a_Wonderful_Life "It's a Wonderful Life")*, 1946), and Darla Hood (*[The Bat](/wiki/The_Bat_(1959_film) "The Bat (1959 film)"),* 1959), but each eventually moved away from acting as a primary career. Several, including [Billie "Buckwheat" Thomas](/wiki/Billie_%22Buckwheat%22_Thomas "Billie \"Buckwheat\" Thomas") and [Eugene "Porky" Lee](/wiki/Eugene_%22Porky%22_Lee "Eugene \"Porky\" Lee"), did not choose to pursue adult roles.

The 1930 *Our Gang* short *[Pups is Pups](/wiki/Pups_is_Pups "Pups is Pups")* was an inductee of the 2004 [National Film Registry](/wiki/National_Film_Registry "National Film Registry") list.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

[E. L. Doctorow](/wiki/E._L._Doctorow "E. L. Doctorow")'s 1975 novel *[Ragtime](/wiki/Ragtime_(novel) "Ragtime (novel)")* ends with the character of Tateh, a Jewish immigrant from Eastern Europe, having a vision of the kind of film he wants to make: "A bunch of children who were pals, white black, fat thin, rich poor, all kinds, mischievous little urchins who would have funny adventures in their own neighborhood, a society of ragamuffins, like all of us, a gang, getting into trouble and getting out again." The implication is that Tateh will go on to produce the *Our Gang* series.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

### Imitators, followers, and frauds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Imitators, followers, and frauds")]

Due to the popularity of *Our Gang*, many similar kid [comedy](/wiki/Comedy "Comedy") [short film](/wiki/Short_film "Short film") series were created by competing studios. Among the most notable are *The Kiddie Troupers*, featuring future comedian [Eddie Bracken](/wiki/Eddie_Bracken "Eddie Bracken"); *[Baby Burlesks](/wiki/Baby_Burlesks "Baby Burlesks")*, featuring [Shirley Temple](/wiki/Shirley_Temple "Shirley Temple"); the *[Buster Brown](/wiki/Buster_Brown "Buster Brown")* comedies (from which *Our Gang* received [Pete the Pup](/wiki/Pete_the_Pup "Pete the Pup") and director [Gus Meins](/wiki/Gus_Meins "Gus Meins")); and *Our Gang's* main competitor, the *[Toonerville Trolley](/wiki/Toonerville_Trolley "Toonerville Trolley")*-based *[Mickey McGuire](/wiki/Mickey_McGuire_(film_series) "Mickey McGuire (film series)")* series starring [Mickey Rooney](/wiki/Mickey_Rooney "Mickey Rooney"). Less notable imitations series include *The McDougall Alley Gang* ([Bray Productions](/wiki/Bray_Productions "Bray Productions"), 1927–1928), *The Us Bunch* and *Our Kids*. There is evidence[[64]](#cite_note-64) that *Our Gang*-style productions were filmed in small towns and cities around the country using local children actors in the 1920s and 1930s. These productions did not appear to be affiliated with Hal Roach, but often used storylines from the shorts of the period, and sometimes went so far as to identify themselves as being *Our Gang* productions.

In later years, many adults falsely claimed to have been members of *Our Gang*. A long list of people, including persons famous in other capacities such as [Nanette Fabray](/wiki/Nanette_Fabray "Nanette Fabray"), [Eddie Bracken](/wiki/Eddie_Bracken "Eddie Bracken"), and gossip columnist Joyce Haber[[65]](#cite_note-65) claimed to be or have been publicly called former *Our Gang* children.[[66]](#cite_note-OGimposters-66) Bracken's official biography was once altered[[66]](#cite_note-OGimposters-66) to state that he appeared in *Our Gang* instead of *The Kiddie Troupers*, although he himself had no knowledge of the change.[[66]](#cite_note-OGimposters-66)

Among notable *Our Gang* imposters is Jack Bothwell, who claimed to have portrayed a character named "Freckles",[[66]](#cite_note-OGimposters-66) going so far as to appear on the game show *[To Tell the Truth](/wiki/To_Tell_the_Truth "To Tell the Truth")* in the fall of 1957, perpetuating this [fraud](/wiki/Fraud "Fraud").[[66]](#cite_note-OGimposters-66) In 2008, a Darla Hood impostor, Mollie Barron, died claiming to have appeared as Darla in *Our Gang*.[[67]](#cite_note-67) Another is Bill English, a [grocery store](/wiki/Grocery_store "Grocery store") employee who appeared on the October 5, 1990, episode of the [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") investigative television [newsmagazine](/wiki/Newsmagazine "Newsmagazine") *[20/20](/wiki/20/20_(US_television_series) "20/20 (US television series)")* claiming to have been Buckwheat. Following the broadcast, Spanky McFarland informed the media of the truth,[[66]](#cite_note-OGimposters-66) and in December, William Thomas, Jr. (son of Billie Thomas, the former child actor who played Buckwheat) filed a [lawsuit](/wiki/Lawsuit "Lawsuit") against ABC for negligence.[[66]](#cite_note-OGimposters-66)

### Persons and entities named after *Our Gang*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Persons and entities named after Our Gang")]

A number of groups, companies, and entities have been inspired by or named after *Our Gang*. The [folk-rock](/wiki/Folk-rock "Folk-rock") group [Spanky and Our Gang](/wiki/Spanky_and_Our_Gang "Spanky and Our Gang") was named for the troupe because lead singer Elaine "Spanky" McFarlane's last name was similar to that of [George "Spanky" McFarland](/wiki/George_%22Spanky%22_McFarland "George \"Spanky\" McFarland"). The band had no connection with the actual *Our Gang* series.

Numerous unauthorized *Little Rascals* and *Our Gang* [restaurants](/wiki/Restaurant "Restaurant") and [day care centers](/wiki/Day_care "Day care") also exist throughout the United States.

## Home media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Home media")]

### 1951–1992: 16 mm and VHS releases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: 1951–1992: 16 mm and VHS releases")]

In the 1950s, home movie distributor [Official Films](/wiki/Official_Films "Official Films") released many of the Hal Roach talkies on [16 mm film](/wiki/16_mm_film "16 mm film"). These were released as *Famous Kid Comedies*, as Official Films could not use *Our Gang*. The company's licensing only lasted for a short period. For years afterward, [Blackhawk Films](/wiki/Blackhawk_Films "Blackhawk Films") released 79 of the 80 Roach talkies on 8mm and [16 mm film](/wiki/16_mm_film "16 mm film"). The sound discs for *Railroadin'* had been lost since the 1940s, and a silent print was available for home-movie release until 1982, when the film's sound discs were located in the MGM vault and the short was restored with sound. As with the television prints, Blackhawk's *Little Rascals* reissues featured custom title cards in place of the original *Our Gang* logos, per MGM's 1949 arrangement with Hal Roach not to distribute the series under its original title. The films were otherwise offered unedited.

In 1983, with the VHS home video market growing, Blackhawk began distributing *Little Rascals* VHS tapes through catalog orders, with three shorts per tape. Blackhawk Films was acquired in 1983 by [National Telefilm Associates](/wiki/National_Telefilm_Associates "National Telefilm Associates"), later renamed [Republic Pictures](/wiki/Republic_Pictures "Republic Pictures"). Republic would release *Little Rascals* VHS volumes for retail purchase as incomplete collections through the rest of the 1980s and early 1990s. By then, all but 11 of the Roach-era sound films were available on home-video formats.

### 1993–2011: Cabin Fever/Hallmark VHS and DVD releases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: 1993–2011: Cabin Fever/Hallmark VHS and DVD releases")]

In 1993, Republic Pictures Home Video sold the home-video rights for the 80 sound Roach shorts and some available silent shorts to Cabin Fever Entertainment, which acquired the rights to use the original *Our Gang* title cards and MGM logos. For the first time in more than 50 years, the Roach sound *Our Gang* comedies were available in their original formats. The first 12 volumes of Cabin Fever's *The Little Rascals* VHS set were released on July 6, 1994, followed by nine more on July 11, 1995, coinciding with the theatrical and home-video releases of Universal's 1994 feature.[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69) Each tape contained four shorts, as well as newly produced introductions by film historian [Leonard Maltin](/wiki/Leonard_Maltin "Leonard Maltin").

With these releases, Cabin Fever made all 80 Roach sound shorts, and four silents, available for purchase unedited with digitally restored picture and sound. On August 26, 1997, a limited-edition volume, *For Pete's Sake*, was released in honor of the series' 75th anniversary with an introduction from original cast member Tommy "Butch" Bond and "Petey", the dog from the 1994 feature. The video contained three previously released shorts and the previously unreleased silent short *[Dog Heaven](/wiki/Dog_Heaven "Dog Heaven")*, and the VHS tape was also available in a gift set with a Pete plush doll.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

Cabin Fever began pressing DVD versions of its first 12 *Little Rascals* VHS volumes, with the contents of two VHS volumes included on each DVD, but the company folded in 1998 before the discs could be released. The *Little Rascals* home video rights were then sold to [Hallmark Entertainment](/wiki/Hallmark_Entertainment "Hallmark Entertainment") in 1999, which released the DVDs without an official launch while cleaning its warehouse in early 2000. Hallmark colorized a few *Our Gang* shorts and released them across eight VHS tapes. Later that year, the first 10 Cabin Fever volumes were rereleased on VHS with new packaging, and the first two volumes were released on DVD as *The Little Rascals: Volumes 1–2*. Two further Hallmark DVD collections featured ten shorts apiece and were released in 2003 and 2005.

From 2006 to 2009, [Legend Films](/wiki/Legend_Films "Legend Films") produced colorized versions of 24 *Our Gang* comedies (23 Roach entries, and the public-domain MGM film *[Waldo's Last Stand](/wiki/Waldo%27s_Last_Stand "Waldo's Last Stand")*), which were released across five *Little Rascals* DVDs. In 2011, Legend Films released black-and-white versions of *Little Rascals* DVDs.

[RHI Entertainment](/wiki/RHI_Entertainment "RHI Entertainment") and [Genius Products](/wiki/Genius_Products "Genius Products") released an eight-disc DVD set, *The Little Rascals–The Complete Collection,* on October 28, 2008.[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-72) This set includes all 80 Hal Roach-produced *Our Gang* sound short films. Most of the collection uses the 1994 restorations, while 16 shorts are presented with older Blackhawk Films transfers, as their remastered copies were lost or misplaced during preparations.[[73]](#cite_note-73)[[74]](#cite_note-74)

On June 14, 2011, [Vivendi Entertainment](/wiki/Gaiam_Vivendi_Entertainment "Gaiam Vivendi Entertainment") rereleased seven of the eight DVDs from RHI/Genius Products' *The Little Rascals-The Complete Collection* as individual releases, including the 80 shorts (replacing the Blackhawk transfers on the previous set with their respective 1994 restorations) but excluding the disc featuring extra features.

### 1980s–2016: MGM/Warner Bros. releases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: 1980s–2016: MGM/Warner Bros. releases")]

During the 1980s and 1990s, MGM released several VHS tapes of shorts and a VHS of the feature *General Spanky*. After video rights for the classic MGM library reverted to their new owner, [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros. "Warner Bros.") (through [Turner Entertainment Co.](/wiki/Turner_Entertainment "Turner Entertainment")), in the late 1990s, four of the MGM *Our Gang* shorts appeared as bonus features on Warner Bros. DVD releases.

In 2009, [Warner Home Video](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Home_Entertainment "Warner Bros. Home Entertainment") released all 52 MGM *Our Gang* shorts in a compilation titled *The Our Gang Collection: 1938–1942* (although it also contains the 1943–44 shorts) for manufacture-on-demand (MOD) DVD and digital download. The set was made available by mail order and digital download as part of the Warner Archive Collection, and via the [iTunes Store](/wiki/ITunes_Store "ITunes Store"). A MOD release of *General Spanky* was also released by Warner Archive on DVD in 2016.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

Many unofficial *Our Gang* and *Little Rascals* home-video collections have been released by distributors, comprising shorts (both silent and sound) that have fallen into the [public domain](/wiki/Public_domain "Public domain").

### 2021–2022: ClassicFlix restorations and releases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: 2021–2022: ClassicFlix restorations and releases")]

ClassicFlix, a company specializing in releasing classic films and TV series on home media, licensed the home video rights to Hal Roach's *Our Gang* sound shorts from the current owners, Sonar Entertainment.[[76]](#cite_note-ClassicFlixNews-76)

An [Indiegogo](/wiki/Indiegogo "Indiegogo") fundraiser campaign was launched to finance extensive restorations of the shorts from original 35mm [nitrate](/wiki/Nitrate "Nitrate") film sources.[[76]](#cite_note-ClassicFlixNews-76) When the campaign did not meet its fundraising goal, other sources of financing were sought for the restorations.[[77]](#cite_note-77) The first ClassicFlix release, *The Little Rascals: The ClassicFlix Restorations, Volume 1,* was released on DVD and Blu-ray on June 1, 2021, featuring the first eleven "talking" short subjects in the series from 1929 and 1930.[[78]](#cite_note-78) Five further volumes followed through June 2022, comprising the rest of the Hal Roach era shorts through 1938 in newly restored versions, as well as *The Little Rascals: The Complete Collection Centennial Edition,* containing all six volumes in DVD and Blu-ray formats, released in November 2022.[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81) In 2025, ClassicFlix released *The Little Rascals: The Restored Silents-Volume One,* which features eight restorations of early silent *Our Gang* shorts with new music scores, including the longest reconstructed version to date of the original 1922 short, *[Our Gang](/wiki/Our_Gang_(film) "Our Gang (film)")*.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

## Status of ownership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Status of ownership")]

Currently, the rights to the *Our Gang*/*Little Rascals* shorts are divided.

[Legend Films](/wiki/Legend_Films "Legend Films") and Multicom Entertainment Group (formerly known as Chicken Soup for the Soul Entertainment and Halcyon Studios, Sonar Entertainment, RHI Entertainment, Cabin Fever Entertainment and Hallmark Entertainment)[[83]](#cite_note-83) co-own the copyrights of and holds the theatrical rights to the 1931-1938 Roach-produced *Our Gang* shorts. Halcyon acquired these after absorbing [Hal Roach Studios](/wiki/Hal_Roach_Studios "Hal Roach Studios") in 1988, and both Roach's estate and Cabin Fever Entertainment in the late 1990s.[[84]](#cite_note-84) The [Crackle](/wiki/Crackle_(service) "Crackle (service)") streaming service, a Chicken Soup for the Soul subsidiary, offered the SD Cabin Fever restorations of the sound era (1929-1938) *Little Rascals* shorts on its service until its closure in 2024 as Chicken Soup for the Soul Entertainment entered [Chapter 7 bankruptcy](/wiki/Chapter_7_bankruptcy "Chapter 7 bankruptcy").[[85]](#cite_note-85) Crackle also provided those shorts to YouTube for streaming with advertisements, where they remain as of 2026.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

In 2021, ClassicFlix acquired the rights to the entire Hal Roach-era *Our Gang* catalog (silent and sound) for home video and streaming release, and completed extensive 2K restorations of all 89 of the sound era (1929-1938) short subjects, by re-scanning from 35mm archival sources, and completing further restoration, [image stabilization](/wiki/Image_stabilization "Image stabilization"), and overall digital picture cleanup. In 2023, they began restorations of the silent-era (1922-1929) *Our Gang* catalog, with any available elements (some from this era are considered [lost films](/wiki/Lost_film "Lost film")).[[87]](#cite_note-87)

[Paramount Skydance](/wiki/Paramount_Skydance "Paramount Skydance") subsidiary [CBS Media Ventures](/wiki/CBS_Media_Ventures "CBS Media Ventures") (through [King World](/wiki/King_World "King World")) owns the rights to the *Little Rascals* trademark and has all other media rights to the 1931–1938 Roach shorts, which constitute *The Little Rascals* broadcast and cable television package, with certain territory exclusions controlled by [Cinematographische Commerz-Anstalt](/w/index.php?title=Cinematographische_Commerz-Anstalt&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cinematographische Commerz-Anstalt (page does not exist)"). CBS offers original black-and-white and [colorized](/wiki/Film_colorization "Film colorization") prints for syndication. The King World/CBS *Little Rascals* package was featured as exclusive programming (in the United States) for [AMC](/wiki/AMC_(TV_channel) "AMC (TV channel)") from August 2001 to December 2003, with child actor [Frankie Muniz](/wiki/Frankie_Muniz "Frankie Muniz") hosting. As part of a month-long tribute to Hal Roach Studios, Turner Classic Movies televised a 24-hour marathon of Roach *Our Gang* shorts – both sound films and silents – on January 4–5, 2011.[[88]](#cite_note-88) Some of the silent *Our Gangs* (such as *Mary, Queen of Tots* and *Thundering Fleas*) resurfaced on TCM at this time with new music scores in [stereo](/wiki/Stereo "Stereo") sound. As of 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit), the sound *Little Rascals* shorts in the CBS package air on the [MeTV](/wiki/MeTV "MeTV") network's spinoff channel [MeTV+](/wiki/MeTV%2B "MeTV+") as part of its *Comedy Classics* block alongside *Laurel and Hardy* and *[The Three Stooges](/wiki/The_Three_Stooges "The Three Stooges")*.[[89]](#cite_note-89)

The MGM-produced *Our Gang* shorts and the rights to the *Our Gang* trademark, as well as the 1936 spin-off film *[General Spanky](/wiki/General_Spanky "General Spanky")*, are owned by [Warner Bros. Discovery](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Discovery "Warner Bros. Discovery") through [Turner Entertainment Co.](/wiki/Turner_Entertainment "Turner Entertainment") The assets were acquired by Turner Entertainment Co. in 1986 when its founder, [Ted Turner](/wiki/Ted_Turner "Ted Turner"), purchased the pre-May 1986 MGM library; Turner merged with the former [Time Warner](/wiki/WarnerMedia "WarnerMedia") in 1996.[[90]](#cite_note-90) The television rights for the MGM *Our Gang* shorts belong to [Warner Bros. Television Distribution](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Television_Studios#Warner_Bros._Domestic_Television_Distribution "Warner Bros. Television Studios"), and the video rights to [Warner Home Video](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Home_Entertainment "Warner Bros. Home Entertainment"). The MGM *Our Gang* shorts today appear periodically on [Turner Classic Movies](/wiki/Turner_Classic_Movies "Turner Classic Movies"). Until its closure in 2018, the MGM *Our Gang* shorts were available for streaming via the subscription-based [Warner Archive Instant](/wiki/Warner_Archive_Collection "Warner Archive Collection") streaming video service.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

Any short released in 1930 or before is public domain, owing to the [copyright law of the United States](/wiki/Copyright_law_of_the_United_States "Copyright law of the United States").

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Our_Gang&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Notable films")]

Main article: [Our Gang filmography](/wiki/Our_Gang_filmography "Our Gang filmography")

The following is a listing of selected *Our Gang* comedies, considered by [Leonard Maltin](/wiki/Leonard_Maltin "Leonard Maltin") and Richard W. Bann (in their book *The Little Rascals: The Life and Times of Our Gang*) to be among the best and most important in the series.

* 1923: *[The Champeen](/wiki/The_Champeen "The Champeen")*, *[Derby Day](/wiki/Derby_Day_(1923_film) "Derby Day (1923 film)")*
